data.title: Robotics News At CES All About Platforms
data.description:
According to most predictions, this year was going to be the breakout year for robotics at CES. And, in fact, that was the case. But while the physical products made the biggest initial splash at the show, it’s the news about robotics platforms and tools that will have the most long-term impact.

Sure, there was a huge number of robotic devices and demonstrations available to see in Las Vegas, but most all of them were not ready for primetime and certainly aren’t going to be commercial products anytime soon. The innovations introduced by chip makers, system builders, IP-focused companies and more, however, will start to make a meaningful difference starting this year. Architectures, reference platforms, software tools and related sub-components built and debuted by companies like Nvidia (NVDA), AMD, Intel (INTC), Qualcomm (QCOM), Arm (ARM), and others are going to enable the coming Physical AI revolution to get off the ground.

Qualcomm kicked things off with the latest edition to its robotics platform, the Dragonwing IQ10 SOC (System on Chip). Designed for the most advanced robotics applications, including the “brains” for humanoid robots, the IQ10 brings together many of the company’s most advanced component technologies. It incorporates up to 18 compute-grade Oryon CPU cores, an Adreno GPU, Hexagon NPU, Spectra ISP (Image Signal Processor), support for up to 20 cameras and more, with a total system-wide TOPS performance number of 700 according to Qualcomm. The IQ10 also incorporates a real-time safety subsystem to help enable support for important functional safety standards, which are going to be extremely critical for most robotics applications.

In addition to the new chip, the company also touted its range of other Dragonwing industrial processors and software tools, noting that the company’s recent acquisition of Arduino allows it to reach an extremely large range of potential robotics developers - from students and enthusiasts to industrial professionals - with a consistent tool chain.

At the Nvidia keynote, CEO Jensen Huang described the entire robotics platform that his company is working to create. From upgraded versions of its Jetson and Thor compute platforms - the new Jetson T4000 is powered by Nvidia’s latest Blackwell GPU architecture - to a whole range of models and training tools, Nvidia laid out an impressive vision for how it’s moving the robotics market forward. What was most noticeable is how much of the company’s efforts in robotics are now focused on software and AI models. The new Cosmos Transfer and Cosmos Predict 2.5 models are the latest iterations of its world models, designed for simulations and training of robots and autonomous machines in real-world environments. Cosmos Reason 2 is Nvidia’s newest VLM (Vision Language Model) and Isaac GROOT N1.6 is a VLA (Vision Language Action) model - arguably the LLM equivalents of the robotics world. They’re both intended to let robots see, understand, and react to objects and actions in the real world, with GROOT specifically focused on full body control for humanoid robots.

Intel’s keynote also made a brief mention of the robotics-related efforts the company is making with an Edge AI-focused version of its new Core Ultra Series 3 (codenamed “Panther Lake”) platform. Though many people don’t immediately think of Intel when it comes to robotics, the company has had a big presence in the industrial robotics market for a very long time. Now, with the dramatically improved GPU capabilities in Core Ultra Series 3 - as well as an enhanced NPU - Intel clearly sees an opportunity to tap into the excitement around physical AI and robotics. Intel’s Robotics AI Suite was recently updated to support Core Ultra Series 3, and the company introduced an industrial-grade Edge AI version of Panther Lake at CES. This marks the first time Intel has done a simultaneous launch of mainstream PC and edge-focused chips, emphasizing its desire to quickly jump into robotics and other Edge AI applications.

At the AMD keynote, CEO Dr. Lisa Su also made a big point to emphasize AMD’s interest in robotics as part of a broad sweeping narrative about how the company’s reach has grown. While there was no discussion about specific robotics-related products, the company did bring an impressive-looking humanoid robot called GENE.01 developed by Italian firm Generative Bionics (recently spun out from the Italian Institute of Technology (IIT)) to the stage that was touted to be powered by AMD technology. Specifically, the robot leverages AMD’s FPGAs for embedded vision and sensor response, as well as AMD CPUs, GPUs and its open-source ROCm software stack. Initially promised by the end of this year, the GENE.01 robot incorporates a number of intriguing innovations, including whole-body tactile sensors - connected to the FPGAs - enabling a touch-sensitive human “skin” to the robot, which is supposed to allow for safer interactions with humans.

The fact that the robot didn’t walk onto or off the stage was a bit disappointing - and suggests that the initial timeline could be a bit overeager. In a separate meeting I had with Generative Bionics CEO Daniele Pucci, however, I learned more about the company’s heritage from IIT and its 20+-year history of building robots that perform a great deal of very precise movements. More importantly, I also started to really appreciate their partnership with AMD and how the experience of working with a company with that kind of robotics history could have a very positive long-term impact on AMD’s future robotic initiatives.

Semiconductor design leader Arm did not make any specific robotics-related product announcements at CES, but it did reveal that the company has recently done a reorganization that pulls together the automotive, IoT, and robotics efforts into a single group, led by industry veteran Drew Henry. In my conversations with Drew, he emphasized Arm’s very long history in robotics, as well as real-time systems, functional safety, microcontrollers, software systems linking all these pieces, and many other elements that are an essential part of building robotics platforms. Needless to say, I’m expecting to hear a lot more about robotics-related initiatives from Arm over the next few years.

What also came up during that discussion was something that I heard from other suppliers as well. Much of the work that many of them have done in the automotive market - particularly with regard to autonomous driving - has obvious relevance and application in robotics. Nvidia’s Jensen Huang has been talking about ADAS as a form of Physical AI for some time, so no surprise there. However, it also became clear that just as smartphone-related technologies can impact PCs (and vice versa), there are significant differences in the expectations and demands around robotics versus autonomous driving. Hence, just finding success in one market does not ensure success in the other. As always, it boils down to having the right combination of hardware, software, platforms, architectures, and other elements in order to create products that the market is willing to accept.

Of course, with robotics, there are still other factors to consider. First, as mentioned earlier, one thing that became abundantly clear at this year’s CES is that the full, widespread commercialization of humanoid-style robots is still many years away - and initial price points are likely to be extremely high. But even beyond these technical and economic concerns, another issue is social acceptance of these devices. Unlike most any other technology product category we’ve ever seen, I think humanoid-style robots are going to face a psychological barrier to adoption that goes well beyond even some of the concerns that have been raised regarding autonomous cars.

Robots in science fiction is one thing, but in everyday real life? Well, that’s another. (And it’s not just me. In numerous conversations around the show, I heard similar sentiments.) Plus, there are enormous physical safety issues around multi-hundred-pound metal devices traipsing through our home and work environments. Personally, I think incremental steps between a robotic vacuum cleaner and real-life Rosie the Robot are going to be essential, but it will be fascinating to see how this market evolves.

Even with these concerns, however, it’s clear that the excitement and anticipation for a robust robotics market is going to drive many important tech innovations over the next few years. It’s also going to create a huge range of opportunities for existing players as well as for startups and other as-yet-unknown companies. As with most things, people will eventually start to get used to robotics devices, so the question won’t be if the robotics market happens, but when.

Disclaimer: Some of the author's clients are vendors in the tech industry.

Disclosure: None.

Original Source: Author

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Why UiPath Is Poised To Outperform In The Age Of AI And Automation
data.description:
UiPath (NYSE: PATH) has done well since I recommended a buy in my March 7, 2025, article on the company. It has increased by 53.59% since then, as of January 7, 2026. In comparison, the S&P 500 (SPX) has risen by 20.87%.

One of the biggest reasons UiPath is outperforming the market is that it is one of the few companies helping its enterprise clients solve a problem that has persisted since chatbots first appeared near the end of 2022 as an experimental concept. Generative Artificial Intelligence (“AI”) and Agentic AI have received significant hype over the last several years. However, AI has not lived up to the hype for many companies.

Agentic AI is an offshoot of generative AI. Companies found that generative AI worked well at performing parlor tricks but was unable to solve their actual problems. The industry invented agentic AI to think autonomously and perform actions on behalf of humans. The goal was to automate complex tasks and make a company’s operations more efficient and less costly. The problem is that agentic AI works well in controlled simulations, solving theoretical problems, but not real-world ones.

When companies deploy various agentic AI solutions in live environments using real data to solve real-world issues, effectiveness and end-user satisfaction have fallen short—a reason some companies are abandoning AI projects.

Gartner published an article on June 25, 2025, titled “Gartner Predicts Over 40% of Agentic AI Projects Will Be Canceled by End of 2027,” which points out the issue: "Over 40% of agentic AI projects will be canceled by the end of 2027, due to escalating costs, unclear business value, or inadequate risk controls."

One of UiPath's major advantages is that it is historically a Robotic Process Automation (”RPA”) company, which is a software that is excellent at performing high-volume, repetitive tasks. One of the big issues that pure agentic AI companies have quickly run into is that performing high-volume, repetitive tasks is not in an AI agent's wheelhouse or core competency. Agentic AI works best with tasks that require some reasoning and choosing options, where the answer doesn’t fit in one neat box.

UiPath’s solution was to combine agentic AI (the reasoning brain) with the brawn of RPA (to provide the heavy lifting on repetitive tasks). So, although you might initially think that companies specializing in agentic AI might have an advantage over UiPath. It may actually be the other way around, and solutions from companies with pure agentic AI solutions may be at a disadvantage. The company’s excellent recent performance may be due to companies recognizing that UiPath offers a superior solution to both newer AI companies and larger AI platforms.

However, the company's move into agentic AI has only occurred over the last several years, and up until recently, UiPath has not done well. In several of my past articles on the company, I noted its execution issues. I also said in my March 7 article, "The company has plenty of long-term upside if it can execute." The stock's rise is due to the market recognizing that the company is beginning to execute.

After founder Daniel Dines returned as CEO (Chief Executive Officer) in early June 2024, he implemented several plans that included a company-wide restructuring that laid off 10% of its workers, revised its sales and marketing strategy, and implemented a strategy to merge Robotic Process Automation (RPA) and agentic Artificial Intelligence ("AI") automation on one platform.

CEO Dines's plans have resulted in the company's second- and third-quarter fiscal year ("FY") 2026 "beat and raise" results, which helped convince the market that the issues that plagued it in calendar year 2024 have stabilized, and the worst may be over.

UiPath's third-quarter FY 2026 revenue increased by 15.9%, representing a re-acceleration from 5% year-over-year revenue growth in the fourth quarter of FY 2025—evidence that CEO Daniel Dines' plan to restore revenue growth is working. The company's third-quarter FY 2026 revenue of $411.1 million beat company projections for between $390 million and $395 million.

The company's third-quarter FY 2026 ARR (Annual Recurring Revenue) increased by 11% to $1.782 billion, beating company projections of $1.771 billion to $1.776 billion.

The market considers UiPath's fourth-quarter revenue forecast as a full-year guidance raise. The company gave its original full-year FY 2026 revenue guidance of $1.571 billion to $1.576 billion in its second-quarter results. When UiPath gave the fourth quarter of its FY 2026 revenue guidance of between $462 million and $467 million in the third quarter, it implied a raised full FY 2026 revenue guidance of $1.592.1 billion to $1.597.1 billion.

It provided FY 2026 ARR guidance of $1.834 billion to $1.839 billion in the second quarter. The company increased its full-year FY 2026 ARR guidance from $1.844 billion to $1.849 billion in the third quarter.

Last, let's discuss profitability: despite the company's increased revenue, the market may be unwilling to award it a higher valuation until it can prove it can grow revenue without increasing costs. UiPath was founded in 2005 and held its Initial Public Offering ("IPO") on April 21, 2021. The company has been unprofitable throughout its public history until recently.

Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Daniel Dines said on the third quarter earnings call, "We are on track to be GAAP profitable for the full year 2026 for the first time." If the company follows through on that promise, it may remove a major element that has been holding back its valuation and stock price since its IPO.

Although UiPath has a solid balance sheet and positive free cash flow ("FCF"), the market didn't like the prospect of its long-term future being in question, since unprofitable companies could eventually run out of cash.

If it reports annual profitability when it announces FY 2026 earnings on March 11, 2026, investors will likely be much more confident in the company's long-term future, which could drive a higher stock valuation.

Aside from UiPath's improved fundamentals, a significant reason to invest in the company is its combination of RPA and agentic AI on a single platform to automate complex business processes, may be slightly ahead of competitors' solutions.

Combining agentic AI and RPA

Chief Financial Officer ("CFO") & Chief Operating Officer ("COO") Ashim Gupta said the following at Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference:

Now dissect what is a process. A process is a series of 20 steps. Within those 20 steps, maybe 30% of them or 40% of them can be done on rule-based, task-based deterministic type steps. Another 40% or 50% of them can be done, but they need reasoning power. It's probabilistic in what needs to happen.

By combining RPA and AI agents, UiPath can automate 70% to 90% of most business processes. The last 10% to 30% is human monitoring of the process. The following image shows UiPath's platform, which illustrates how it combines agents, people, and robots to automate processes across industries and departments.

The middle of the image contains the word "Orchestration," which refers to a cloud-based platform named by UiPath as Maestro that organizes work among AI agents, humans, and robots.

Maestro distinguishes UiPath from some pure agentic AI competitors. Agentic AI has several significant issues, including hallucinations and security concerns. Suppose you task an agentic AI with performing a financial task, such as hypothetically managing an investment portfolio, and the AI hallucinates a solution based on how it believes the stock market works; it could mismanage the portfolio, leading to disastrous financial results. The worst part is that pure agentic AI can operate as a black box, making it hard for the user to determine exactly why the AI produced such poor results. Orchestration acts like the guardrails against a wayward AI agent.

The orchestrator may assign a robot to re-examine the AI agent’s assumptions, and if the RPA software finds that the AI agent violated a stock market “rule,” it may refer the decision-making process to a human. The possible mistakes that an AI agent might make are why it is essential to keep humans in the loop.

So how does the orchestrator know which rules the AI agent needs to comply with? UiPath has a tool called DMN (Decision Model and Notation) that allows users to convert complex rules into a decision table for either the orchestrator or the RPA to follow, rather than only relying on an AI agent’s decision, which may be based on out-of-date or biased training data. As a result, the orchestrator and RPA software govern when the AI agent can act and ensure its decisions remain within the bounds of defined rules. UiPath is among the first companies in the market to think along these lines, as shown by its introduction of Maestro, which it billed as the "industry's first agentic orchestration platform."

CFO and COO Ashim Gupta said at the Barclays 23rd Annual Global Technology Conference:

Process orchestration means now you have a 15 -- a 20-step process. Realistically, it's a 200-step process. You have humans, robots and agents working together, how are you going to monitor it? How are you going to govern that? How are you going to visualize it? That is what Maestro is, a product that we GA-ed earlier this year that allows companies to do that.

The above Ashim Gupta description is the simplistic explanation of what Maestro does. The following image shows some of the "in the weeds" features of Maestro:

When UiPath could only provide companies with RPA, or software robots, to complete 30% to 40% of a task, it was not worth automating specific processes for some companies. However, by automating 70% to 90% of processes, many more customers will be interested, thereby increasing UiPath's revenue-generating opportunities.

Risks

The ultimate promise of agentic AI is that UiPath sells AI agents for a premium. The risk for investors is that the "premium-pricing" future may take longer than expected to materialize.

Although agentic AI sounds exciting, the technology may not produce meaningful revenue growth immediately. Wolfe Research analyst Arsenije Matovic pressed management during the third-quarter FY 2026 Q&A, asking when the company would start seeing revenue from agentic AI and Maestro. Specifically, Matovic asked:

You doubled the number of customers developing agents quarter-over-quarter and Maestro process instances. What's the uplift you've seen in those customers? And is it kind of fair to assume that into next year as that revenue ramps, it can help sustain that NRR [Net Revenue Retention] and offset some of that low-end weakness that we've seen?

CFO and COO Gupta gave the following response:

We will continue to contend, but we're in the early innings of agentic...In terms of direct scalable, direct monetization place from agentic. We're -- at this we'll update everybody as we kind of get into next year around assumptions there. But for the immediate short term, there's nothing material as we cited in the script.

Currently, the company expects only indirect benefits from Agentic AI, in the form of selling more RPA and Orchestration, rather than benefiting directly from premium agentic AI sales. If the company fails to generate sufficient indirect revenue while waiting for the agentic AI market to become ready for prime time (meaning it becomes directly monetizable), the market may become impatient with the lack of increased revenue despite the AI hype and sell the stock off.

Although AI agents sound exciting, UiPath and others could find it hard to turn the technology into a profitable business because when UiPath builds an AI agent for a company, it is a bespoke solution. The problem with bespoke solutions is that the solutions are hard to replicate across the market. Chief Executive Officer ("CEO") Daniel Dines said on the third quarter FY 2026 earnings call:

We have deployed our teams in various use cases across various industries. We see some particular partners emerging in health care like, for instance, in revenue cycle management, prior authorization, claims management, in financial services, financial crimes, it's -- but again, I think it's a bit too early to mention one particular use case where we see like great replicable potential.

That statement means that, as of today, AI agents don't have a standard use case, and UIPath cannot use predictable, repeatable processes across its customer base to solve a problem. Instead, the company must create client-specific solutions, which makes the solutions non-scalable.

The problem is that software/cloud is highly profitable only when solutions have scalability, meaning the software/cloud architect (UiPath) can build a solution (AI agent) for a use case and sell it many times to other customers with similar problems or sell it across other use cases. Up until now, every agentic AI implementation has required engineers and support personnel for that specific customer, incurring a human-intensive cost higher than the cost of pure software or cloud services. Until management proves that "agentic AI has replicable use cases," the margins for providing AI agents will likely be lower than those of RPA robots.

The last risk that I will discuss is the competitive environment in the enterprise automation industry. Although several open-source RPA programs, such as Robot Framework, and open-source agentic platforms, such as LangChain or LlamaIndex, are technically competitors, UiPath's biggest competitive factors are companies with greater name recognition and platforms with greater scale. Examples of such companies include hyperscalers Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (GOOGL) (GOOG), and Amazon (AMZN), as well as platform providers Salesforce (CRM) and ServiceNow (NOW).

Although UiPath may technically have a better agentic AI framework compared to companies like Microsoft and Salesforce and may have a first-mover advantage in combining RPA, agentic AI, and orchestration, these companies are not standing still. Microsoft, in particular, has invested heavily in RPA over the last several years, and both Salesforce and Microsoft have invested in orchestration. If these larger competitors quickly catch up, it may stunt UiPath’s revenue growth and profitability.

Some of these larger companies can offer existing customers a bundled solution that essentially makes AI agents and RPA solutions free, which may outweigh UiPath's technical superiority in RPA and agentic AI, especially if customers believe the hyperscalers' AI agents and robots are "just good enough." If the collective competition from the big boys reduces UiPath's revenue growth or margins below investor expectations, the stock may lose its market-beating potential.

Valuation

UiPath has a trailing 12-month ("TTM") price-to-sales (P/S) of 5.934, well above its March 7, 2025, P/S ratio of 4.777, but almost equal to its three-year median of 5.937, a sign of a fair valuation.

The following table compares UiPath's forward P/S ratio to estimated revenue growth and profitability with those of other companies it competes against in RPA and agentic AI.

Typically, in these comparisons, investors consider the company with the highest P/S or P/S/G ratio overvalued relative to companies with lower P/S/G ratios. Since UiPath's P/S (5.48) and P/S/G (0.60) ratios exceed the mean P/S (5.205) and P/S/G (0.525) ratios, some investors may conclude that the market overvalues UiPath relative to its peer group of RPA and agentic AI companies.

If you include TTM profitability indicators, UiPath exceeds the group's median gross margin but lags the group's median FCF, operating margin, and ROIC. Some investors might conclude that its current lackluster profitability makes it undeserving of a premium P/S and P/S/G valuation.

Therefore, some investors may consider the stock overvalued, while others may consider it fairly valued, depending on whether they focus on its P/S/G valuation or its profitability.

UiPath's non-GAAP price-to-earnings (P/E) was 26.24, above the Information Technology sector median of 25.31, suggesting overvaluation.

The company's two-year forward P/E to estimated EPS growth ("PEG") ratio is 1.79, calculated by dividing its FY 2027 forward P/E of 23.50 by its 2027 EPS growth rate of 13.12%. Typically, growth investors consider a PEG ratio between 1.0 and 2.0, but once it exceeds 2.0, they consider it overvalued.

As a result, growth investors may consider UiPath fairly valued, with upside potential. Suppose the stock was at a PEG ratio of 2.0 today; the price would be $19.94, an 18% rise above its January 8 closing price of $16.88.

Although some might argue that UiPath is a mature company, given its longstanding Robotic Process Automation (RPA) business with well-established customers, it is still in its growth phase and investing heavily in developing a platform to support AI agents and is better classified as a growth company. Typically, when a company is in a heavy investment phase, its FCF may decline or become erratic, making FCF-based valuations inaccurate. So, I will refrain from making assessments of measurements such as FCF yield or price-to-FCF or from performing a discounted cash flow analysis.

UiPath is Still a Buy

UiPath has an annualized standard deviation of monthly returns of 57.34%, which is 4.5 times greater than the S&P 500 (SPX). Some may consider its volatility as a sign of a speculative stock.

Investors who prioritize capital preservation and predictable returns should avoid this stock. Although the company has produced solid results over the last several quarters, it still deals with major execution risk and uncertainty about how some of its end markets, such as agentic AI, will develop.

Although the stock may have high risk, it also has high potential reward. The company is already a key participant in the RPA market. Precedence Research estimates the RPA market at $28.31 billion in 2025, growing at a compound annual growth rate ("CAGR") of 24.20% to reach around $247.34 billion by 2035.

UiPath's move into the agentic AI market has the potential for even more explosive growth; Fortune Business Insights estimates the agentic AI market at $8.03 billion in 2025 and forecasts it to grow from $11.78 billion at the end of 2026 at a 46.61% CAGR to $251.38 billion by 2034.

At the end of the third quarter, the company generated $1.553 billion in TTM revenue, 4.2% of the combined RPA and agentic AI total addressable market ("TAM") of $36.62 billion, giving it plenty of room to grow into its end markets.

UiPath's quarterly and annual revenue growth of 15.92% and 9.30%, respectively, significantly lag the RPA and agentic-AI estimated CAGRs of 24.20% and 46.61%, respectively.

The big risk with this company is that it fails to become a major participant in the agentic AI market. Suppose UiPath's agentic AI initiatives fail to play out favorably, and its competition erodes its position in the RPA and agentic AI market. In that case, annual revenue growth may remain in the 8% to 12% range, and growth investors may sell off the stock.

Investors who buy the company today are speculating that the company can build a scalable agentic AI solution and use Maestro to fight off competition from giants like Microsoft and Salesforce to become the "Operating System" of the enterprise, taking over functions such as ERP (Enterprise Resource Planning), CRM (Customer Relationship Management), and older legacy systems with agentic AI and RPA solutions. If this high-risk strategy plays out favorably and the company's existing clients adopt Maestro, the tailwinds from growth in the RPA and agentic AI markets could reaccelerate annual revenue growth to the 15% to 18% range. If the company really does well and UiPath becomes the primary orchestrator for the $251 billion Agentic AI market, revenue growth may rise to the 25% to 30% range. The higher the revenue growth, the more investors would raise the stock's valuation.

Overall, I believe the base case for this stock is annual revenue growth in the 15% to 18% range because I believe Microsoft and other larger companies will capture a significant chunk of the agentic AI market and prevent UiPath from completely dominating. If it reaches a 15% to 18% annual revenue range, it will handily beat analysts' FY 2026 revenue forecasts of 11.51% and 9.21% in FY 2027 and would be deserving of a premium valuation.

Additionally, if it continues to become more profitable, investors may award it a FY 2027 premium PEG ratio valuation of 2.0 or above, suggesting at least 18% upside above its January 8 closing price.

I recommend buying UiPath, but only for risk-tolerant, aggressive growth investors.

data.title: VBF: BBB Spreads Are Too Low (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the Invesco Bond Fund (VBF) during the market dislocation of April/May 2025, when we assigned the name a 'Buy' rating. The CEF is up since that rating, but we find investment grade bonds currently stretched, prompting a review of VBF and the performance of investment grade names in general in the past year. The article will articulate a forward for the CEF as well in light of the current macro.

What does the fund hold?

VBF is a CEF that is mostly concentrated on investment grade corporate bonds:

  • AAA: 0.12%
  • AA: 7%
  • A: 27%
  • BBB: 45%
  • BB: 15%
  • B: 5%
  • other: residual

The CEF has a small HY sleeve of roughly 20%, but its main risk factors generally lie with investment grade bonds. From a ratings perspective the CEF is concentrated on A and BBB bonds, categories which account for roughly 72% of the CEF holdings. This translates into A and BBB spreads being the most important aspect to watch for this name.

The fund is very granular, with over 1,200 holdings and a duration of 6.9 years. Additional analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $0.19 bil
  • Expense ratio: 0.56%
  • Duration: 6.9 years
  • Distribution rate: 5.4%
  • Discount: -6.8%
  • Z-score: -1.5%

As any fixed rate bond fund, VBF is helped by lower rates and tighter spreads. However, as we will see shortly, spreads are at historic lows, thus raising a red flag here.

Performance

Since mid April 2025, VBF had a very similar total return to the iShares iBoxx Investment Grade Corporate Bond ETF (LQD):

Both funds posted a total return close to 8%, although VBF has a lower duration. The outperformance in VBF from a duration stand-point can be explained by a wider than normal discount to NAV during the market sell-off.

Longer term the picture is also very similar:

Please note the high correlation between the two instruments throughout time, with VBF being more volatile on account of its discount and premium to NAV moves.

Discount/Premium to NAV

Any CEF structure will have a premium or discount to its net asset value, usually quite correlated with the overall fund performance:

We can see from the above chart a violent move wider in the discount during the April 2025 market risk-off event, valuation aspect which prompted us a 'Buy' rating at the time in conjunction with spread moves. After seeing the discount re-bound, we are now seeing another widening driven by market uneasiness around credit spreads. Let us have a look.

Distributions are mostly supported

This CEF does not create high unsupported distribution rates. As per its latest Section 19a notice, the income is mostly supported by cash-flows:

One could also see that from the low distribution rate for the CEF, figure which is not used as a marketing tool. The CEF has kept this stance in the past few years, and we appreciate the NAV stability generated by such an option.

Credit spreads are historically low

As we said in the above sections, the CEF is overweight BBB bonds which drive the overall performance. Historically speaking BBB spreads are very low:

U.S. BBB credit spreads are now sub 1%. Yes, you read that correctly. In fact, spreads are the lowest in over 11 years as of today after moving to 1.5% during the April 2025 event. We can see that historically they usually fluctuate around 1.5%, with recessions driving them higher to 2%, while complacent markets moving them lower.

As an investor you want to buy an instrument like VBF when spreads are high because you end up being compensated for market turmoil. That is what we did in our last piece, where the April 2025 risk-off event represented an interesting entry point into the fund. Right now spreads are too tight and unappealing.

The Fed is set to lower rates but the market is already pricing this

An aspect which many institutions bring to fore is the fact that the Fed is set to lower rates, thus bond investors are set to benefit from the duration bump (remember that there is an inverse relationship between bonds and rates). While we agree that the Fed is set to lower rates, we would point out that the market has already priced this in by lowering the 7-year rates:

Please note the interesting relationship between Fed Funds and 7-year rates. These intermediate duration rates often front-run the Fed, both during monetary tightening and easing. Also, during stable monetary times we see the 'term premium' of the 7-year point over Fed Funds. Rates have already moved lower anticipating lower Fed Funds, and if we take 3% as neutral Fed Funds and add a term premium, we will end up somewhere around 3.5% to 3.7%, quite close to where they are now.

Our view is that for 3% neutral Fed Funds, the market has already priced that in, thus there is no 'duration bump' to be had here. If we get a much more aggressive Fed that moves rates much lower, the CEF could benefit from that duration pull. But that is not our base case, nor what the market is pricing in.

Why 'Hold' and not 'Sell'

While we are apprehensive about very tight spreads, we have to acknowledge that the CEF has moved towards the bottom of its range for its discount, thus appearing balanced by these two factors. Also a very aggressive Fed could change the risk/return profile for the fund. We are therefore turning neutral on this CEF, not finding the current price point appealing, but also seeing a scenario where there could be some sort of upside outside the fund yield.

Conclusion

VBF is a fixed income CEF. The name focuses on A and BBB corporate bonds, and has done very well in the past year. With BBB spreads very tight historically and the Fed cuts already priced in, we are downgrading the CEF from 'Buy' to 'Hold'.

data.title: Synthetic Risk Transfers Are The Talk Of The Town. But Are They As Scary As They Look?
data.description:
By Alfonso Ricciardelli, CFA, CAIA

Synthetic risk transfers (SRTs) have recently started raising eyebrows. First introduced in Europe in the early 2000s as a niche form of regulatory capital optimization, they have since evolved into one of the most important tools in modern bank balance sheet management.[1]

Since 2016, banks have executed SRTs referencing more than $1.1 trillion in underlying assets, with annual issuance worth tens of billions of dollars. As activity has climbed and as private credit funds have eagerly absorbed the contracts, regulators and financial journalists have grown increasingly vocal about their concerns.

The question is whether this scrutiny is warranted.

What are SRTs?

SRTs are a form of synthetic securitization, often called “on-balance-sheet securitization,” in which a bank offloads a portion of a loan portfolio’s credit risk through a contract, typically a credit derivative or guarantee, without fully selling or removing the loans from its balance sheet.

In Europe, where the market was born, the investor typically acquires mezzanine loan risk by selling (writing) a credit default swap (CDS) and, in the United States, through a credit-linked note (CLN). The primary protection sellers are public and private credit funds, which are attracted by competitive yields, access to high-quality diversified credit exposures, and the ability to tailor risk via tranches. Banks pay for this protection because it allows them to transfer part of their loan risk to investors, which in turn reduces their regulatory capital requirements and frees up capital for new lending at a lower cost than raising equity.

The originating bank retains the first loss (junior) tranche[2]. The investor, who does not have specific knowledge of the pool’s underlying loans (only generic details like maturity, ratings, and industry), earns a fixed premium or coupon. If defaults in the portfolio occur, the bank absorbs the first loss while the investor covers losses up to the mezzanine tranche limit.

The bank retains the client relationship, loan administration, and interest income to maintain “skin in the game,” which is a regulatory requirement. But since it shed a portion of the portfolio risk, the bank is permitted to reduce capital against the loans.

SRTs are typically engineered for capital relief and risk management. On the former, Basel capital rules are widely viewed as excessively penalizing certain assets. For example, auto loans require disproportionately high capital despite extremely low default rates. SRTs allow banks to reduce risk-weighted assets (RWAs) by 50% to 80% in many transactions. In addition, by transferring risk without shrinking their balance sheets, banks can reduce geographic, borrower, or sector concentration risk.

Where SRTs Are Growing and Why

European banks remain the most active issuers, accounting for roughly 60% to 70% of global issuance. The market has its roots in Europe because it is a heavy bank-centric loan market with a stringent interpretation of post global financial crisis (GFC) capital regulations. A clear supervisory framework and a deep investor base in Europe have also supported its growth. Each SRT transaction undergoes European Central Bank/European Banking Authority review, and recent regulatory rules have rewarded high-quality structures with more efficient capital treatment.

In the United States, following the Federal Reserve’s 2023 guidance recognizing direct CLN structures as eligible for capital relief, banks quickly entered the market. The United States now represents nearly 30% of global deal flow. In Asia, institutions in markets such as Australia and Singapore have experimented with SRT-like structures, often under different labels or pilot programs, though volumes are considerably smaller.

Born of Overregulation, Yet Heavily Scrutinized

Despite their benefits, SRTs continue to draw significant regulatory scrutiny. Supervisors are most focused on rollover risk, investor concentration, and back-leverage, all of which can become more pronounced as issuance grows.

First, rollover risk arises because SRTs usually mature in three to five years, while the underlying loans often remain on the balance sheet for much longer. If market conditions worsen when an SRT comes up for renewal, banks may struggle to replace the protection, leading to a sudden increase in RWAs and potential pressure to deleverage.

Second, this risk is amplified by investor concentration: a relatively small group of private credit funds dominates the mezzanine market. Their outsized role means that the entire SRT ecosystem depends on the willingness of a handful of players to refinance. In a stressed market, these funds could demand sharply higher spreads or pull back altogether, leaving banks with limited alternatives.

Third, regulators are attuned to back-leverage. Under Basel III/IV and regional rules (e.g., the European Union’s Capital Requirements Regulation), a bank must prove that a material share of the portfolio has been transferred, that the transfer is real, and investors can be protected even under stressed market conditions.

By requiring evidence of material risk transfer and bank skin in the game, the rules aim to prevent regulatory arbitrage through circular transactions and ensure that SRTs strengthen, rather than weaken, the resilience of the financial system.

Finally, concerns about opacity persist. While SRTs are far more standardized and transparent than pre-2008 collateralized debt obligations, their bespoke nature and limited public disclosure still make some observers uneasy about assessing the true distribution of risk.

Eye on the Ball

For banks, SRTs have become a strategic lever to manage capital, mitigate credit exposure, and keep lending volumes intact as the regulatory environment tightened after the GFC.

The public skepticism that surrounds SRTs is, in my opinion, a result of PTSD from the financial crisis. The main difference this time is that moral hazard is meaningfully lower than in pre-2008. Banks retain first-loss exposure, investors hold real risk, and the overall market remains relatively small.

Rather, SRT issuance is a response to overly conservative risk weights that, in the years following the crisis, pushed banks to limit lending. It is a rational approach to redistributing risk and freeing capital for investment, especially in Europe, where banks are by far the dominant player. To institutional investors, SRTs offer potentially differentiated credit exposure and compelling yield.

[1] SRTs are also referred to as “Significant Risk Transfers.” The significant part refers to meeting regulatory criteria (like Basel rules) to get capital relief (reducing required capital) by proving enough risk has truly transferred, while synthetic highlights the risk is transferred via derivatives (like CDS) rather than selling the asset itself (a cash securitization).

[2] In the US, the bank usually retains the first loss junior tranche and transfers the senior risk (only two tranches in the transaction).

Disclaimer: Please note that the content of this site should not be construed as investment advice, nor do the opinions expressed necessarily reflect the views of CFA Institute.

data.description:
• The Fund (Investor Class)(OAKWX) underperformed the benchmark, the MSCI World Index, for the quarter, the calendar year and since inception.
  • At the sector level, the largest contributors to performance were health care and financials, while consumer discretionary and consumer staples were the largest detractors.
  • Geographically, the top three regional allocations are 55.6% in the U.S., 31.4% in Europe ex U.K. and 9.5% in the U.K. For the quarter, the U.S., Germany and Denmark were the top contributors to performance. China, the Netherlands and France the largest detractors from performance. Emerging markets accounted for 3.6% of the portfolio.

Fourth-quarter highlights

Top contributors

Top Contributor | Detractor

Top contributor

Bayer was the top contributor during the quarter. Two anticipated events developed in the company's favor. First, Bayer enjoyed a positive readout on the company’s stroke drug, Asundexian, which met its primary endpoint in a recent phase III trial. Asundexian has the potential to be a blockbuster and support a return to growth for the pharmaceuticals business, in our opinion. Second, the United States Solicitor General recommended that the Supreme Court hear Bayer’s appeal in Durnell v. Monsanto, increasing the odds that the RoundUp matter is heard by the Court. Both events support our investment thesis for Bayer as management works to turn around fundamental performance and contain litigation risk.

Top detractor

Alibaba Group was the top detractor during the quarter. The China-headquartered technology conglomerate’s stock price declined as it reported mixed results. The core E-commerce business continues to perform well, and Cloud revenue growth is accelerating. However, the company’s earnings were negatively impacted due to significant spending on subsidies to grow their Quick Commerce business, in our opinion. We believe losses from Quick Commerce will be reduced over time and continue to believe the company is well-positioned for long-term growth, having been one of the early investors in Chinese AI. Over time, we think it can leverage its advanced capabilities and leading market position to unlock further value.

Portfolio Activity

New purchases

Sanofi is a global pharmaceutical company developing biologics, vaccines and healthcare solutions to prevent and treat a wide range of conditions in immunology, hemophilia, rare diseases and general medicine. The company is best-known for its blockbuster drug, Dupixent, a fast-growing biologic that provides doctors with a single, versatile therapy for multiple inflammatory conditions, combining strong efficacy with a clean safety profile. Beyond Dupixent, we think there is a lot to be excited about as management has prioritized innovation by aggressively investing in research and development to cultivate a pipeline of promising products. Despite strong fundamentals, the stock has been weighed down by a volatile year for the vaccine market, investor concerns about patent cliffs and minimal value ascription to its pipeline. This created the opportunity for us to invest in a company we believe is improving, with a long runway for future growth as vaccine markets normalize and new product potential is realized.

data.title: Cerrado Gold: A Deep Value Bet
data.description:
I believe many of those reading about this stock know about the Lassonde Curve system. I believe Cerrado Gold (CRDOF) is stuck in an orphan period. The stage when the initial euphoria of the PEA stage has faded and financing evaluation has not yet begun.

In my opinion, the market is punishing Cerrado exactly for being in a capital-intensive vacuum. The current price shows that investors are not valuing Mont Sorcier's $1.6 billion NPV as an asset but more like a problem, which will need a lot of financing. However, we already see the first moves towards an institutional de-risking phase. The transition from the 2022 PEA to the upcoming Q2 2026 BFS study is a critical moment I am looking forward to.

Most of the time, when projects are transitioning from speculation (PEA) to bankable (BFS) status, the discount to NAV starts shrinking. Mont Sorcier has high-grade magnetite concentrate (67%+ Fe), which gives technical security, which many "orphan" projects don't have. Buying stock at this moment, investors take the position before institutional capital, which enters into the market only with confirmation (most of the time BFS).

My First Thesis

In the first thesis, I have conservatively said that the stock price should be around $1.28. My thesis was that Argentina operations will pull the company out of the liquidity crisis. As the stock reached the price I was looking into, I decided to check whether my thesis still stands.

Earnings showed that Minera Don Nicolas not only reached production goals but also was able to manage AISC growth against the backdrop of Argentine inflation. This confirms my original thesis that MDN is not a ballast but a cash generation engine, which now directly finances Portugal and Canada projects. Numbers show that net loss is going down; EBITDA, in fact, is stabilizing, and for a microcap company, this is a good de-risking sign. MDN generated $11.8 million adjusted EBITDA in a quarter, so we can say that operational cash flow became positive.

My biggest fear was $30 million of current liabilities. However, they successfully restructured part of the debt and agreed to defer payments using MDN gold as collateral. This is still a risky part about Cerrado, as it did reduce the risk of bankruptcy, but still it has a lot of potential to become a real issue in the future. The market is still pricing this risk as existential, but I believe it's now time to look at it as operational.

Last earnings proved that the company can survive on its own. I believe that my first thesis of a stock price around $1.28 was just the company coming to life. If everything moves smoothly from now on (which never happens), I believe that the company can be traded at around $9 per share.

Valuation

Cerrado valuation in the market is a classical example of information asymmetry and mispricing of assets (fun fact, my master's research was exactly about this topic and niche). Traditional indicators such as P/E or EV/EBITDA are, let's call it, completely worthless and misleading in cases like this. Because most of the company's value is not in current cash flows from Argentina but in a huge "paper" asset in Canada - Mont Sorcier project.

I have used the SOTP (sum of the parts) method to evaluate this project. This lets us isolate each asset and see its true value, without needing to valuate the whole company based on quarterly results.

Why am I modeling $100 million dilution?

I have made this mistake in the past, where I take the current net asset value (NAV) and divide it by the current outstanding shares. This creates the illusion of "Blue Sky" potential, which in reality might not exist. I have heard an interesting quote: "The value in assets is not the value in the bank".

My model is conservative; I use the assumption that Cerrado will have to dilute current shareholders by at least $100 million to finance Mont Sorcier construction and finish its BFS. Why? Because it's "derisked NAV". Even with the assumption that shareholders will be diluted by about 33-50%, diluted NAV is still above 9 dollars, when the market is valuing the company at $1.28. This shows that the market is not pricing in the possible dilution but bankruptcy, which is strange and I believe we can trace it to microcap inefficiency.

Mont Sorcier Factor

In my model, the biggest weight comes from the 2022 PEA, which valued the project at $1.6 billion. The market currently is discounting this fact because, let's be honest, PEA is the lowest reliability study. But I believe we have an arbitrage opportunity, as in Q2 2026 we should see BFS, which will change the status of this project from speculation to a bankable project. Historically, transitions from the PEA to BFS stage are the stages where the value of mining companies rises most rapidly, as the project becomes funded. I model this, as my strategy is to buy before the news, not after.

Risks asymmetry

Why am I modeling 0.5x NAV and not 1.0x NAV? To be fair, I have never seen even the best companies being valued at full asset value. Mainly due to operational risks and commodity price swings. However, even using a realistic 0.5x NAV and including dilution, I still get a 300-400% higher price than the current one. This means that the current market is pessimistic towards this company, as even a conservative scenario can make huge returns. I think this is a classical deep value situation; the risk is limited (because the assets are real), and the potential is huge.

Portfolio Building and Trading

This part of the analysis I always write when I am about to start trading the stock in question, as it is part of my process. Due to the stock being a microcap and me saying it is a strong buy, it is necessary to talk about how to mitigate trading risks, as I cannot deem it as an investment but more of a trade. NAV just shows the goal, but quantitative analysis shows the road the investor will have to walk. I have done econometric analysis of Cerrado behaviour, with 1177 observations.

I have done standard investment GARCH (1,1). Why? Because the risk in mining stocks is not evenly distributed, it is "sticky". The GARCH analysis showed that the persistence of volatility reaches 0.98, which means that periods of calm are replaced by extremely sharp jumps. For traders, this is a good signal that the position should be formed before the explosion of volatility, and it's risky to chase the price that has already jumped (like in the last days).

My analysis also confirmed that CRDOF is very sensitive to gold (GLD) price changes; I know it's obvious. Regression showed a 1.69 beta, which means that holding the stock in the short term is like having leverage on gold. Quantile regression results show that so-called "boom beta" jumps to 2.11. This is critical for portfolio building. Cerrado has a tendency to move a lot exactly when gold reaches new highs. In contrast, "crash beta" is lower (1.42), indicating asymmetric advantage but still a risky play.

Iron ore price is also a factor. It is a statistically significant relation to the iron ore sector (PICK). Beta is 0.71. Even though the market is still valuing CRDOF through gold, this relation indicates that Mont Sorcier potential is forming in the prices as well. Although, I am quite skeptical about this part of my model.

CRDOF does not have cointegration with major ETFs (like GDX or SIL). Let's call it a holy grail. This means that in the long term the share price is not tied to the general and often stagnant sector average. Its movements are mostly caused by internal events like BFS studies, expansions, and so on. This also gives a possibility to make that alpha that everyone seeks and not just follow sectors beta.

I know that many people are not active traders, so I have also checked the correlation with the US dollar, as it is a recent topic. UUP Beta is -2.76. This makes CRDOF ideal for portfolio building if you want to hedge yourself against the dollar going down. If we combine my SOTP analysis with this, we can see that CRDOF is not only "cheap" stock but technically quite strong.

Summary

The latest results show that $4 million quarterly EBITDA in Argentina is gradually eliminating bankruptcy risk and is financing the expansion. My main thesis is now leading towards the Mont Sorcier project. I believe that my conservative SOTP model shows undervaluation and fundamental potential. Still, the risks are there, and I believe that before investing, investors should be prepared for roller coasters. Also, please take my analysis with a grain of salt; I am opening a position myself and rating this stock as a strong buy.

data.title: FCG: A Look At The Structure And Suitability Of This Energy Equity Index ETF
data.description:
The First Trust Natural Gas ETF (FCG) is an exchange-traded index fund that attempts to focus somewhat more heavily on natural gas producers than other energy indices. However, it still exhibits a high correlation to broader energy indices and, as such, might not offer much in the way of diversification for a portfolio that already has exposure to the energy sector. The fund might still make sense for investors who are trying to profit from a thesis that revolves specifically around natural gas.

Basic Overview of the First Trust Natural Gas ETF

The First Trust Natural Gas ETF is an exchange-traded index fund that aims to track the price and yield performance of the ISE-Revere Natural Gas Index. The name of the index suggests that it may be a commodity index that mechanically tracks a strategy involving natural gas futures, but that would be an incorrect assumption. In fact, the index is an equity index that tracks the performance of a collection of upstream exploration and production energy companies that derive most or all of their revenues from the production of natural gas. According to the index’s webpage:

The ISE REVERE Natural Gas Index is designed to track public companies that derive a substantial portion of their revenues from midstream activities and/or the exploration and production of natural gas.

As might be expected, this is a fairly focused index due to the simple fact that many companies that produce natural gas also produce crude oil. In many cases, crude oil and natural gas are found alongside one another in hydrocarbon reservoirs, so companies that extract resources from wells drilled into these reservoirs end up producing both substances. We can therefore expect the index to include several companies that are actually producing and selling both crude oil and natural gas.

As is the case with most exchange-traded equity index funds, the strategy that is employed by the First Trust Natural Gas ETF is to buy the equities of all of the companies that are contained in the index in the same proportion of its portfolio as is included in the index. The website for the index, perhaps surprisingly, does not include a list showing the weightings of the largest constituents in it. It does, however, provide a page that lists all 38 index components in alphabetical order. The fund’s website does provide the weightings of the ten largest holdings in its portfolio, though. Here they are:

Many of these names are likely to be familiar to anyone who follows the energy sector somewhat closely. After all, a few of these companies are among the largest energy companies in the United States. For example, here are the market capitalizations of each of these companies as of January 12, 2026:

Company Name
